lecture_context.lecture_title: Stanford CS231N Lecture 11: Large-Scale Distributed Training

unit.name: HFU and MFU: the optimization metric for distributed training
unit.description: Defines hardware and model FLOPs utilization, shows typical numbers, and motivates MFU as the main knob-selection target.
unit.summary: Before introducing the remaining parallelism strategies, the lecture defines two throughput metrics. Hardware FLOPs Utilization (HFU) is the fraction of the device's theoretical dense-matmul throughput achieved by a benchmark, and on a simple H100 matrix-multiply benchmark the number rises to about 80% for large matrices [ts=3503s] [ts=3569s]. Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) is stricter: it asks what fraction of theoretical device FLOPs are spent on the model's forward/backward work once data loading, communication, activation checkpointing, optimizer steps, and other overheads are included [ts=3589s] [ts=3663s]. The instructor presents MFU as the main guiding metric for tuning distributed training because it compresses many system-level decisions into a single throughput target [ts=3687s] [ts=3697s]. As rough heuristics, MFU above 30% is good, above 40% is excellent, and published Llama3 large-scale runs achieve MFUs in the high-30s to low-40s across 8k-16k GPUs [ts=3700s] [ts=3735s]. The segment also notes a counterintuitive trend: newer GPUs can produce worse MFU because compute throughput is scaling faster than communication bandwidth, making communication increasingly dominant [ts=3742s] [ts=3778s].

key_points.text: HFU is the fraction of theoretical dense-matmul throughput that a device actually realizes on a benchmark.
A straightforward H100 matmul benchmark reaches about 80% HFU for large matrix sizes around 8k by 8k.
MFU measures what fraction of theoretical FLOPs are used for the model's forward/backward work in the real training loop.
MFU can be estimated by comparing theoretical forward/backward time from model FLOPs to measured iteration time.
MFU above 30% is good and above 40% is excellent for modern large-scale training.
Llama3 reports MFUs in roughly the high-30s to low-40s on 8k-16k GPU runs.
More recent GPUs can show worse MFU because communication bandwidth improves more slowly than compute throughput.

kp_catalog.name: Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU)
kp_catalog.description: A system-level efficiency metric measuring how much of a device's theoretical compute is turned into useful model forward/backward work in the actual training loop.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) clarifies a system-level efficiency metric measuring how much of a device's theoretical compute is turned into useful model forward/backward work in the actual training loop, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
